mainText:
**Have ICE Reforms Worked?**

**Blanche Testifies For AG Confirmation Hearing**
Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche testified before the Senate Judiciary Committee Wednesday as part of his confirmation hearings to serve as the permanent attorney general. Over five hours, Blanche faced questions from both Democrats and Republicans regarding his tenure as head of the Justice Department after President Donald Trump removed Pam Bondi. Republican Senator John Cornyn questioned Blanche about his involvement in attempting to create a $1.8 billion anti-weaponization fund for victims of alleged Justice Department persecution. Blanche dismissed the fund as a “moot issue,” since the DOJ had claimed in June that it would no longer proceed with it. He also acknowledged “mistakes that were made” during the handling of the Jeffrey Epstein files and apologized to victims of sexual abuse whose information was exposed by the documents.

With Lindsey Graham’s recent death and Senator Mitch McConnell’s hospitalization, Blanche’s nomination will likely require every Republican on the Judiciary Committee to vote “yes” to move it to full consideration by the Senate. If not confirmed, he could continue in an “acting” capacity indefinitely.

**Clayton Testifies for National Intelligence Role**
Trump’s nominee for director of national intelligence, U.S. Attorney for the Southern District of New York Jay Clayton, also testified for his confirmation hearing on Wednesday, where he faced questions about election security. Clayton, who would replace acting DNI Bill Pulte, refused to assert that Joe Biden won the 2020 presidential election, stating only that Biden was “certified” as president. He also faced questions over previous claims about election integrity and whether a White House official had requested him to subpoena several *New York Times* journalists regarding a national security investigation. Last Friday, Clayton subpoenaed four *Times* reporters, ordering them to appear before a Manhattan grand jury on Wednesday—the same day as his own hearing—over reporting that Trump’s new Air Force One, a gift from Qatar, lacked missile-defense systems its predecessor carried.

Senate Intelligence Committee Chair Tom Cotton also asked Clayton to reaffirm his commitment to downsizing the oversight agency. Clayton responded that the Office of the Director of National Intelligence should function as a “board of directors” for the intelligence community, focusing more on oversight than ongoing operations.

**Trump Reverses ICE Pause on Traffic Stops**
President Donald Trump called on Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) to continue conducting traffic stops, reversing a directive issued the previous day by Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin that paused most such operations after the fatal shootings of two motorists in Texas and Maine. In a Truth Social post, Trump characterized traffic stops as “one of I.C.E.’s most important and effective” tools, urging agents to be “judicious, fair and smart.” The earlier pause, which exempted criminal warrants and joint operations, followed the deaths of Joan Sebastián Durán Guerrero in Maine and Lorenzo Salgado Araujo in Texas, neither of whom was the intended target of the operations. The shootings have drawn scrutiny from lawmakers and renewed criticism of ICE tactics, particularly the lack of body cameras during the Maine operation. A third man died Tuesday in St. Augustine, Florida, after a tractor-trailer struck him while he fled from federal immigration agents. Since Trump returned to office last year, at least ten individuals have died during encounters with immigration agents.

**Republicans Unveil Budget Resolution**
House Republicans revealed a $95 billion legislative plan Wednesday, focusing on funding defense, supporting farmers, and imposing stricter voter registration rules in line with the provisions of the SAVE America Act. The majority of the funding, $73 billion, is designated for military and intelligence efforts, while $12 billion is allocated for farm assistance to help growers facing higher fuel and fertilizer costs. The resolution outlines an additional $10 billion for election measures and mandates proof of citizenship for voter registration. The budget framework does not include offsets for the new spending but calls for the respective committees to create legislation
